fact: Pyth is a pull oracle, so prices are only updated on-chain when a user or dApp requests them.
Pyth is the only oracle that uses first-party data directly from 100+ exchanges and market makers.
On Solana, Pyth prices update every ~400ms, matching the chain's block time.
The primary utility of the PYTH token is on-chain governance via the Pyth DAO.
Pyth delivers real-time data to 55+ different blockchains.
Pyth does not rely on nodes scraping websites; it relies on the people who actually create the trades.
A price of $100 ± $0.05 means Pyth is 95% certain the true price is within that 10-cent range.
The Pythians is the official name for members of the Pyth Network community.
No single entity controls Pyth price feeds; they are aggregated from multiple independent sources.
Every Pyth price update is stored on-chain and is permanently auditable.
The pull model is designed to be more gas-efficient than traditional push models.
Major dApps like Synthetix, Venus, and Drift use Pyth to secure billions in value.
The original Pyth whitepaper was released in 2021 to address latency in DeFi.
To become a Pyth publisher, an institution must prove access to proprietary trade data.
Pyth provides a smoothed EMA price to help dApps avoid liquidations during tiny wicks.
Pyth's aggregation logic and code are open-source and available on GitHub.
Hermes is a web service that lets users pull Pyth prices to EVM or Move-based chains.
Pyth's mission is to make every world financial price available on every blockchain.
Pyth was originally built on Solana for its high-speed, low-cost infrastructure.
Pyth uses cryptographic signatures to prove data came from specific publishers.
Pyth provides extended-session pricing for US stocks like TSLA and NVDA.
Pyth provides real-time feeds for Gold, Silver, and Crude Oil.
Pyth powers fast settlement for large prediction markets like Polymarket.
Pyth supports liquid staking tokens like jitoSOL and mSOL for DeFi collateral.
During extreme crashes, Pyth confidence intervals widen to prevent stale or false data.
Pyth Pro is a premium, institutional data suite for ultra-high-frequency feeds.
Pyth supports FX pairs like EUR/USD and JPY/USD.
Pyth uses Wormhole to move price data from Pythnet to other chains.
Pyth provides prices for Treasury Bills and other tokenized real-world assets.
Pyth Benchmarks allow historical point-in-time price lookups.
Pyth supports 500+ price feeds across crypto, stocks, and metals.
Pyth provides real-time data for indices like the S&P 500 and NASDAQ-100.
If a publisher stops sending data, Pyth marks the feed as stale.
Developers can set slippage rules based on deviation between publishers.
Because Pyth aggregates on Pythnet, the price is identical across all supported chains.
Entropy V2 provides verifiable on-chain randomness that cannot be predicted.
Entropy uses a commit-reveal process to secure the random number.
Pyth Lazer enables ultra-low-latency oracle updates as fast as 50ms.
Pyth uses a median-based aggregation that ignores outlier prices.
Pythnet is a custom app-chain dedicated to processing oracle data.
Pyth weights publishers with higher historical accuracy and lower latency.
Pyth Lazer lets developers choose a consensus threshold (e.g., 3 or 10 publishers).
Outlier prices far from the median are automatically ignored.
Pyth's final price is confidence-weighted, not a simple median.
Pyth tracks publisher latency to keep data delivery fast.
Pyth remains accurate even if one-third of publishers go offline or send bad data.
Users can verify Pyth prices via a Merkle proof on Pythnet.
Entropy V2 is used for NFT minting, RPG combat, and fair lottery draws.
Every Pyth feed has a unique 32-byte hex ID for the asset.
Pyth sources data from institutions like Jane Street, Virtu, and HRT.
